root.narration: You see that the opponent's queen on d8 is the only piece defending the e8 square. Your rook on e1 wants to get to e8 for a back rank checkmate, but the queen defends that square. The black king on g8 is boxed in by its own bishop on f8 and knight on h8, plus the pawns on f7, g7, h7. If you could DEFLECT the queen away from defending e8, your rook could deliver checkmate! What is your plan?

choices.text: Play Qf6! Threaten Qxg7 checkmate! The queen on d8 must leave its post to deal with the mate threat, and then Re8 is checkmate!
Play Re8 directly to try for back rank mate.
Move a pawn forward and build up slowly.

outcome-great.narration: You play Qf6! Threatening Qxg7 checkmate! The opponent's queen on d8 is the only piece that can stop it. If the queen takes your queen (Qxf6), then Re8 is back rank checkmate because the queen left the defense of e8! The king is trapped by its own bishop on f8, knight on h8, and pawns. If the queen moves to e7 to defend g7, you play Qxe7 and then Re8 is still checkmate. The queen cannot do two jobs at once -- defend e8 AND stop checkmate on g7. This is deflection at its finest!

outcome.headline: DEFLECTION! The Queen Cannot Do Two Jobs!
outcome.explanation: Deflection is a brilliant tactic where you force a defending piece to abandon its post. The opponent's queen was doing two critical jobs: defending e8 (preventing back rank mate) and guarding g7 (preventing Qxg7 mate). By threatening Qxg7 checkmate, you forced the queen to deal with that threat, which means it can no longer defend e8. The key to deflection is finding a piece that is overworked -- trying to protect multiple things -- and creating a threat it MUST respond to, leaving the other thing undefended.
outcome.whatToRemember: Look for overworked pieces! If one enemy piece is doing two defensive jobs, create a threat that forces it to abandon one job.

keyTerms.definition: Forcing a defending piece to move away from what it is guarding, leaving that target unprotected. Like luring a guard away from the gate!
A piece that is defending two or more things at once. It cannot do both jobs, so you can exploit this weakness!
Creating two threats at the same time so the opponent cannot deal with both. Deflection creates a dual threat!

outcome-okay.narration: You push a pawn and build up your position. Your opponent uses the time to reorganize and move the queen to a square where it defends everything. The chance for deflection is gone. The game is still equal but the winning shot has vanished.

outcome.headline: Building Up, But the Tactic Was There!
outcome.explanation: Improving your position slowly is usually a good plan, but not when a winning tactic is available right now! The opponent's queen was overloaded defending both g7 (from Qxg7 mate) and e8 (from Re8 back rank mate). By threatening Qxg7 mate with Qf6, you could have forced the queen to abandon the e8 defense. Always scan for overworked pieces before settling for quiet moves. Tactics are time-sensitive -- they disappear if you do not act!
outcome.whatToRemember: Before making slow moves, check if the opponent has any overworked pieces. A tactical shot might be available right now!

keyTerms.definition: Constantly scanning the position for tricks, traps, and forced sequences. Do not miss hidden tactics!

outcome-bad.narration: You play Re8 directly, going for back rank mate. But the opponent's queen captures your rook! Qd8xe8. You just gave away a rook for nothing. The queen was still defending e8 and you walked right into it. You needed to deflect the queen FIRST, then go for the back rank.

outcome.headline: Rook Captured! You Forgot the Defender!
outcome.explanation: Before making a tactical move, always check if the target square is defended! The e8 square was guarded by the queen on d8. You needed to force the queen away from defending e8 FIRST (that is the deflection), and THEN play Re8. The correct sequence was Qf6 (threatening Qxg7 mate), which forces the queen to deal with the mate threat, and only then Re8 becomes checkmate. Order of operations matters!
outcome.whatToRemember: Do not attack a defended square! First deflect the defender away, THEN strike. Remove the guard before attacking!

keyTerms.definition: A square that is protected by an enemy piece. Do not move to a defended square without first dealing with the defender!
A move that sets up your main idea. Sometimes you need a deflection or decoy BEFORE the final blow.
